### **1. Direct Answer**
Manufacturers of **connected medical devices** under the **MDR 2017/745** and **GDPR 2016/679** must comply with **parallel but distinct** data protection obligations. Under **GDPR**, they act as **data controllers** (or joint controllers) for personal data processed by the device, including **special categories of data (health data)**. Under **MDR**, they must integrate **data protection by design and by default** into the device’s safety and performance requirements, ensure **cybersecurity**, and report incidents involving personal data breaches. The two frameworks **overlap** in areas like risk management and transparency but **do not substitute** each other.

---

### **2. Step-by-Step Analysis**

#### **Step 1 — Actor Status (per regulation)**
- **GDPR (Article 4(7))**: The manufacturer is a **"controller‘controller’ means the natural or legal person [...] which determines the purposes and means of the processing of personal data‘manufacturer’ means a natural or legal person who manufactures or fully refurbishes a device [...] and markets that device under its name or trade mark‘data concerning health’ means personal data related to the physical or mental health of a natural personProcessing of personal data revealing [...] data concerning health [...] shall be prohibitedManufacturers shall establish, implement, document and maintain a system for risk managementProcessing shall be lawful only if [...] the data subject has given consent [...] or processing is necessary for the performance of a contractTaking into account the state of the art [...] the controller shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measuresDevices shall be designed and manufactured in such a way as to protect, as far as possible, against unauthorised accessManufacturers shall establish, implement, document and maintain a system for risk managementManufacturers shall report [...] any serious incident involving devices made available on the Union marketManufacturers shall periodically verify the correctness of all of the data relevant to devices they have placed on the marketThe person responsible for regulatory compliance shall [...] ensure that the reporting obligations referred to in Articles 87 to 91 are fulfilledHealth Dataraw data" as health data**: Even **sensor data** may qualify under GDPR Article 4(15).

results.answer:
> ASSESSMENT STATUS — Provisional legal qualification assessment. This answer should be read as a case-specific compliance analysis, not as an automatic status determination.

> ⚠ **FAITHFULNESS FLAG** — 1 of 1 verbatim quote(s) could not be matched against the retrieved regulatory context and have been removed from the answer. Treat the underlying point as unverified until checked against the source provisions.
> - “:** - The AI Act does **not** define what is likely to constitute a”

### **1. Explicitly stated in retrieved text**

#### **Actor Status**
- **Under the AI Act (AIA):**
 - You are the **provider** of the AI system per **Article 3(3) AIA**:
 > ‘provider’ means a natural or legal person, public authority, agency or other body that develops an AI system or a general-purpose AI model or that has an AI system or a general-purpose AI model developed and places it on the market or puts the AI system into service under its own name or trademark, whether for payment or free of charge;
 - This status applies **from inception** (first deployment, even internally) and does **not** depend on external distribution.

- **Under the MDR:**
 - You are the **manufacturer** of the medical device per **Article 2 MDR**:
 > ‘medical device’ means any instrument, apparatus, appliance, software, implant, reagent, material or other article intended by the manufacturer to be used, alone or in combination, for human beings for one or more of the following specific medical purposes...
 - The MDR explicitly states that **software shall also be deemed to be an active device** (Article 2 definition).

#### **Classification Route**
- **AI Act High-Risk Classification:**
 - Your AI system is **high-risk under Article 6(1) AIA** (Route I) because:
 1. It is **software as a medical device** (covered by **MDR 2017/745**, listed in **Annex I Section A AIA**).
 2. The device is **Class IIb**, which **requires third-party conformity assessment** under the MDR (Article 52 MDR).
 - **Article 6(1) AIA** states:
 > Irrespective of whether an AI system is placed on the market or put into service independently of the products referred to in points (a) and (b), that AI system shall be considered to be high-risk where both of the following conditions are fulfilled:
 > (a) the AI system is intended to be used as a safety component of a product, or the AI system is itself a product, covered by the Union harmonisation legislation listed in Annex I;
 > (b) the product whose safety component pursuant to point (a) is the AI system, or the AI system itself as a product, is required to undergo a third-party conformity assessment, with a view to the placing on the market or the putting into service of that product pursuant to the Union harmonisation legislation listed in Annex I.
 - **Route II (Annex III AIA) does not apply** unless your system also performs a biometric, essential-service, or other Annex III function (not indicated in the question).

- **MDR Classification:**
 - Your device is **Class IIb** (stated in the question). This classification is **independent of the AI Act** but **determines** the AI Act high-risk status via Article 6(1) AIA.

#### **Trigger Event**
- **Placing on the market or putting into service** of the medical device (MDR) or the AI system (AIA) activates the obligation chains.
 - **MDR Article 2 definitions**:
 > ‘placing on the market’ means the first making available of a device, other than an investigational device, on the Union market;
 > ‘putting into service’ means the stage at which a device, other than an investigational device, has been made available to the final user as being ready for use on the Union market for the first time for its intended purpose;
 - **AIA Article 3 definitions**:
 > ‘placing on the market’ means the first making available of an AI system or a general-purpose AI model on the Union market;
 > ‘putting into service’ means the supply of an AI system for first use directly to the deployer or for own use in the Union for its intended purpose;

#### **Legal Effects**
##### **MDR Obligations (Class IIb)**
- **Conformity Assessment:**
 - Class IIb devices require **third-party conformity assessment** (Article 52 MDR). For software, this typically involves **Annex IX (quality management system + technical documentation assessment)** or **Annex XI (production quality assurance)**.
 - **Annex IX, Section 4 (technical documentation assessment)** applies, including:
 > The notified body shall verify that the clinical evidence and the clinical evaluation are adequate and shall verify the conclusions drawn by the manufacturer on the conformity with the relevant general safety and performance requirements. That verification shall include consideration of the adequacy of the benefit-risk determination, the risk management, the instructions for use, the user training and the manufacturer's post-market surveillance plan, and include a review of the need for, and the adequacy of, the PMCF plan proposed, where applicable. (**Annex IX, 4.6**)
 > Based on its assessment of the clinical evidence, the notified body shall consider the clinical evaluation and the benefit-risk determination, and whether specific milestones need to be defined to allow the notified body to review updates to the clinical evidence that result from post-market surveillance and PMCF data. (**Annex IX, 4.7**)
 > The notified body shall, in circumstances in which the clinical evidence is based partly or totally on data from devices which are claimed to be equivalent to the device under assessment, assess the suitability of using such data... (**Annex IX, 4.5**)

- **Post-Market Surveillance (PMS) and Post-Market Clinical Follow-Up (PMCF):**
 - **Article 10(9) MDR** requires a **PMS system**, and **Annex XIV Part B** mandates **PMCF** for all devices:
 > PMCF shall be understood to be a continuous process that updates the clinical evaluation referred to in Article 61 and Part A of this Annex and shall be addressed in the manufacturer's post-market surveillance plan. When conducting PMCF, the manufacturer shall proactively collect and evaluate clinical data from the use in or on humans of a device which bears the CE marking and is placed on the market or put into service within its intended purpose as referred to in the relevant conformity assessment procedure, with the aim of confirming the safety and performance throughout the expected lifetime of the device, of ensuring the continued acceptability of identified risks and of detecting emerging risks on the basis of factual evidence. (**Annex XIV, Part B, 5**)

- **Continuous-Learning Specifics:**
 - The MDR does **not** explicitly address continuous-learning models, but **Annex IX, 4.7** and **Annex XIV Part B** imply that **any changes to the device (including model updates) must be assessed for impact on clinical evidence and benefit-risk determination**.
 - **MDCG 2025-6 (non-binding guidance)** states:
 > The MDR, IVDR and AIA require a risk management system that is a continuous iterative process throughout the entire lifecycle of the device, including in both the pre-market and post-market phases.

##### **AI Act Obligations (High-Risk AI System)**
- **Conformity Assessment:**
 - **Article 43(3) AIA** states:
 > For high-risk AI systems covered by the Union harmonisation legislation listed in Section A of Annex I, the provider shall follow the relevant conformity assessment procedure as required under those legal acts. The requirements set out in Section 2 of this Chapter shall apply to those high-risk AI systems and shall be part of that assessment.
 - This means:
 1. You **must comply with the MDR conformity assessment procedure** (e.g., Annex IX for Class IIb).
 2. The **AI Act requirements (Chapter III, Section 2)** are **integrated into the MDR conformity assessment** (e.g., risk management, data governance, technical documentation, transparency, human oversight).
 3. **Points 4.3, 4.4, 4.5, and the fifth paragraph of 4.6 of Annex VII AIA** also apply (these relate to technical documentation, risk management, and post-market monitoring).

- **Key AI Act Requirements (Chapter III, Section 2):**
 - **Risk Management System (Article 9 AIA):**
 > A risk management system shall be established, implemented, documented and maintained in relation to high-risk AI systems... The risk management system shall be understood as a continuous iterative process planned and run throughout the entire lifecycle of a high-risk AI system, requiring regular systematic review and updating.
 - This **explicitly covers continuous-learning models** and requires **ongoing monitoring and mitigation of risks** (e.g., bias, performance drift, cybersecurity threats).
 - **Data and Data Governance (Article 10 AIA):**
 > High-risk AI systems which make use of techniques involving the training of AI models with data shall be developed on the basis of training, validation and testing data sets that meet the quality criteria referred to in paragraphs 2 to 5 whenever such data sets are used.
 - For continuous-learning models, this implies **ongoing data quality checks** and **bias mitigation** during post-market use.
 - **Technical Documentation (Article 11 AIA + Annex IV):**
 - Must include **detailed information on the AI system**, including:
 > A description of the changes to the high-risk AI system and its performance that have been pre-determined by the provider at the moment of the initial conformity assessment and are part of the information contained in the technical documentation. (**Annex IV, 2(f)**)
 - For continuous-learning models, this means **documenting all pre-determined updates** (e.g., retraining is likely to trigger, performance thresholds).
 - **Human Oversight (Article 14 AIA):**
 > High-risk AI systems shall be designed and developed in such a way, including with appropriate human-machine interface tools, that they can be effectively overseen by natural persons during the period in which the AI system is in use.
 - This is **critical for continuous-learning models** to ensure **human review of updates** and **intervention in case of drift or errors**.
 - **Accuracy, Robustness, and Cybersecurity (Article 15 AIA):**
 > High-risk AI systems shall be designed and developed in such a way that they achieve, in the light of their intended purpose, an appropriate level of accuracy, robustness and cybersecurity, and perform consistently in those respects throughout their lifecycle.
 - For continuous-learning models, this requires **ongoing monitoring of accuracy and robustness** and **cybersecurity measures to prevent adversarial attacks** (e.g., data poisoning).
 - **Post-Market Monitoring (Article 72 AIA):**
 > Providers of high-risk AI systems shall establish and document a post-market monitoring system in a manner that is proportionate to the nature of the AI technologies and the risks of the high-risk AI system.
 - This **overlaps with MDR PMS** but adds **AI-specific monitoring** (e.g., performance drift, bias, cybersecurity incidents).

- **Continuous-Learning Specifics:**
 - **Article 43(4) AIA** states:
 > High-risk AI systems that have already been subject to a conformity assessment procedure shall undergo a new conformity assessment procedure in the event of a substantial modification, regardless of whether the modified system is intended to be further distributed or continues to be used by the current deployer.
 - **Subparagraph 2** clarifies:
 > For high-risk AI systems that continue to learn after being placed on the market or put into service, changes to the high-risk AI system and its performance that have been pre-determined by the provider at the moment of the initial conformity assessment and are part of the information contained in the technical documentation referred to in point 2(f) of Annex IV, shall not constitute a substantial modification.
 - This means:

Under the **EU AI Act**, a **provider of a general-purpose AI model with systemic risk** has **two layers of obligations**:

1. **Core obligations applicable to all general-purpose AI models** (Article 53)
2. **Additional obligations specific to general-purpose AI models with systemic risk** (Article 55)

---

### **1. Core Obligations (Article 53)**
These apply to **all providers of general-purpose AI models**, including those with systemic risk:

#### **(a) Technical Documentation (Article 53(1)(a))**
- **Obligation**: Draw up and keep up-to-date **technical documentation** of the model, including its **training and testing process** and **evaluation results**.
- **Content**: Must include, at a minimum, the information set out in **Annex XI** (e.g., model architecture, training data, computational resources, evaluation strategies).
- **Purpose**: Provide this documentation **upon request** to the **AI Office** and **national competent authorities**.
- **Quote**:
 > *"draw up and keep up-to-date the technical documentation of the model, including its training and testing process and the results of its evaluation, which shall contain, at a minimum, the information set out in Annex XI for the purpose of providing it, upon request, to the AI Office and the national competent authorities"* **[Article 53(1)(a)]**

#### **(b) Transparency for Downstream Providers (Article 53(1)(b))**
- **Obligation**: Draw up, keep up-to-date, and **make available** information and documentation to **providers of AI systems** who intend to integrate the general-purpose AI model into their systems.
- **Content**: Must enable downstream providers to:
 - Understand the **capabilities and limitations** of the model.
 - Comply with their own obligations under the AI Act.
- **Minimum requirements**: Information set out in **Annex XII** (e.g., intended tasks, acceptable use policies, input/output modalities, integration instructions).
- **Quote**:
 > **[Article 53(1)(b)]**

#### **(c) Copyright Compliance (Article 53(1)(c))**
- **Obligation**: Put in place a **policy to comply with Union law on copyright and related rights**, including:
 - Identifying and complying with **reservations of rights** expressed under **Article 4(3) of Directive (EU) 2019/790** (e.g., opt-outs by rightsholders for text and data mining).
 - Using **state-of-the-art technologies** to detect and respect such reservations.
- **Quote**:
 > **[Article 53(1)(c)]**

#### **(d) Public Summary of Training Data (Article 53(1)(d))**
- **Obligation**: Draw up and **make publicly available** a **sufficiently detailed summary** about the **content used for training** the model.
- **Format**: According to a **template provided by the AI Office**.
- **Quote**:
 > *"draw up and make publicly available a sufficiently detailed summary about the content used for training of the general-purpose AI model, according to a template provided by the AI Office"* **[Article 53(1)(d)]**

#### **(e) Cooperation with Authorities (Article 53(3))**
- **Obligation**: Cooperate as necessary with the **Commission** and **national competent authorities** in the exercise of their powers under the AI Act.
- **Quote**:

### **Direct AnswerNo, it is not lawful under the EU AI Act to deploy an AI system that infers employees' emotions from webcam feeds during work.**
The prohibition is governed by **Article 5(1)(f) of the EU AI Act**, which explicitly bans the placing on the market, putting into service, or use of AI systems to infer emotions of natural persons in the workplace, except for medical or safety reasons.

---

### **Regulatory Grounding**

#### **1. Prohibition under Article 5(1)(f) EU AI Act**
The EU AI Act **explicitly prohibits** the use of AI systems to infer emotions in the workplace:
> **"the placing on the market, the putting into service for this specific purpose, or the use of AI systems to infer emotions of a natural person in the areas of workplace and education institutions, except where the use of the AI system is intended to be put in place or into the market for medical or safety reasons;"**
> [12] Article 5(1)(f) — EU AI Act [LEGISLATION] [BINDING]

This provision is **absolute** for workplace deployments unless the system is used for **medical or safety reasons** (e.g., monitoring fatigue in professional drivers to prevent accidents).

---

#### **2. Definition of "Emotion Recognition Systeman AI system for the purpose of identifying or inferring emotions or intentions of natural persons on the basis of their biometric data.There are serious concerns about the scientific basis of AI systems aiming to identify or infer emotions, particularly as expression of emotions vary considerably across cultures and situations, and even within a single individual. Among the key shortcomings of such systems are the limited reliability, the lack of specificity and the limited generalisability. Therefore, AI systems identifying or inferring emotions or intentions of natural persons on the basis of their biometric data may lead to discriminatory outcomes and can be intrusive to the rights and freedoms of the concerned persons. Considering the imbalance of power in the context of work or education, combined with the intrusive nature of these systems, such systems could lead to detrimental or unfavourable treatment of certain natural persons or whole groups thereof.special category" of personal data). In a workplace setting, **consent is rarely considered freely given** due to the power imbalance between employer and employee, making it an invalid legal basis.
- **Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU:** The use of emotion recognition systems in the workplace could infringe on **Article 7 (respect for private life)**, **Article 8 (protection of personal data)**, and **Article 31 (fair and just working conditions)**.

However, **the EU AI Act's prohibition is standalone and does not require additional violations of other laws to apply.**

While the EU AI Act itself is **binding**, the following interpretive points from the **preamble (recitals)** provide additional clarity on the scope of the prohibition:
1. **Recital (18)** clarifies that the prohibition **applies to systems that infer emotions from biometric data**, including facial expressions captured via webcam.

The deployment of an AI system that infers employees' emotions from webcam feeds during work is **unlawful under Article 5(1)(f) of the EU AI Act**. This prohibition is **absolute** for workplace use unless the system is deployed for **medical or safety reasons**, which does not include general productivity or performance monitoring. ### **Direct Answer**
A change to an already-deployed high-risk AI system counts as a **"substantial modificationSubstantial Modification"**
The AI Act **formally defines** "substantial modification" in **Article 3(23)**:
> 

**Key criteria:**
- **Unforeseen/unplanned** in the initial conformity assessment.
- **Affects compliance** with Chapter III, Section 2 (e.g., risk management, data governance, transparency, human oversight, accuracy, robustness, cybersecurity).
- **Modifies the intended purpose** (e.g., new use case, expanded scope).

#### **2. Conformity Assessment TriggerArticle 43(4)** mandates a new conformity assessment for substantial modifications:
> *"High-risk AI systems that have already been subject to a conformity assessment procedure shall undergo a new conformity assessment procedure in the event of a substantial modification, regardless of whether the modified system is intended to be further distributed or continues to be used by the current deployer."*

**Implications:**
- **No exceptions**: Applies even if the system remains with the same deployer.
- **Full reassessment**: The provider must repeat the conformity assessment procedure (e.g., internal control under Annex VI or notified body involvement under Annex VII).

#### **3. Pre-Determined Changes Are ExemptArticle 43(4), second subparagraph** explicitly carves out pre-determined changes:
> 

**Requirements for exemption:**
- Changes must be **identified and documented** in the **technical documentation** (Annex IV, point 2(f)) at the time of initial conformity assessment.
- Changes must be **pre-determined** (e.g., planned updates, retraining protocols, or adaptive learning parameters).
- The provider must **anticipate and describe** how the system will evolve post-deployment.

**Example:**
- A medical AI system designed to improve diagnostic accuracy over time through federated learning may include pre-determined retraining cycles in its technical documentation. These cycles would not trigger a new conformity assessment if they were foreseen and documented upfront.

#### **4. Cross-Reference to Technical DocumentationAnnex IV, point 2(f)** requires the technical documentation to include:
> 

This ensures transparency and traceability for pre-determined changes.

---

### **Balanced Analysis: When Is a Change Likely Substantial?**
| **Factor** | **Likely Substantial** | **Likely Non-Substantial** |
|--------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|
| **Planning** | Unforeseen at initial conformity assessment. | Pre-determined and documented in Annex IV. |
| **Impact on Compliance** | Affects Chapter III, Section 2 requirements. | No material impact on compliance. |
| **Intended Purpose** | Modifies or expands the intended purpose. | Purpose remains unchanged. |
| **Learning Post-Deployment** | Uncontrolled or unanticipated learning. | Pre-defined learning protocols (e.g., retraining schedules). |
| **Risk Profile** | Increases risk to health, safety, or fundamental rights. | Risk profile remains within initial assessment bounds. |

**Case-by-Case Assessment Required:**
- The AI Act does not provide an exhaustive list of substantial vs. non-substantial changes. Providers must **document their reasoning** (Article 6(4)) and be prepared to justify their assessment to national competent authorities.
- **MDCG guidance (non-binding)** may offer interpretive examples, but the **binding legal standard** is the definition in Article 3(23) and the exemption in Article 43(4).

---

### **Legal Effects of a Substantial Modification**
If a change is deemed substantial:
1. **New conformity assessment required** (Article 43(4)).
2. **Updated technical documentation** (Annex IV).
3. **Revised EU declaration of conformity** (Article 47).
4. **Reaffixed CE marking** (Article 48).
5. **Re-registration in the EU database** (Article 49).

Failure to comply constitutes a **non-conformity** and may trigger **market surveillance actions** (Article 79) or **penalties** (Article 99).

---

### **Key Takeaways for Providers**
1. **Document pre-determined changes** in the technical documentation (Annex IV) to avoid triggering a new conformity assessment.
2. **Assess all post-deployment changes** against the Article 3(23) criteria (unforeseen + compliance impact or purpose modification).
3. **Maintain records** of the assessment process (Article 6(4)) to demonstrate compliance if challenged.
4. **Monitor MDCG guidance** (non-binding) for examples, but **defer to the binding Regulation** for legal obligations.

As a **deployer** of a third-party high-risk AI system for CV screening in recruitment (Annex III, point 4(b) of the AI Act), your obligations under **Article 26** and related provisions are **distinct from the provider’s** and focus on **safe use, human oversight, input data quality, monitoring, and transparency**. ### **2. Deployer Obligations (Article 26, AI Act)**
#### **A. Use in Accordance with Instructions for Use (Article 26(1))**

 > *"Deployers of high-risk AI systems shall take appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure they use such systems in accordance with the instructions for use accompanying the systems."* **[26] Article 26(1)**

#### **B. Human Oversight (Article 26(2))**
- Assign **natural persons** with **competence, training, and authority** to oversee the AI system’s operation. These individuals must have the **support** needed to intervene or override the system if necessary.
 > *"Deployers shall assign human oversight to natural persons who have the necessary competence, training and authority, as well as the necessary support."* **[26] Article 26(2)**

 > *"To the extent the deployer exercises control over the input data, that deployer shall ensure that input data is relevant and sufficiently representative in view of the intended purpose of the high-risk AI system."* **[26] Article 26(4)**

 > *"Deployers shall monitor the operation of the high-risk AI system on the basis of the instructions for use... Where deployers have reason to consider that the use of the high-risk AI system... may result in that AI system presenting a risk... they shall... inform the provider or distributor and the relevant market surveillance authority."* **[26] Article 26(5)**

#### **E. Log Retention (Article 26(6))**

 > **[26] Article 26(6)**

#### **F. Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment (Article 27)**

 - Public authority, or

 > **[27] Article 27(1)**

#### **G. Registration (Article 49(3) and Annex VIII, Section C)**

 > *"Deployers of high-risk AI systems that are public authorities... shall comply with the registration obligations referred to in Article 49."* **[26] Article 26(8)**
 > **[5] Annex VIII, Section C**

#### **H. Transparency to Affected Persons (Article 50(3))**

1. **Review the Provider’s Documentation**:

2. **Conduct a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment (FRIA)**:

3. **Implement Human Oversight**:

4. **Monitor and Report Incidents**:

5. **Register Your Use (If Applicable)**:

6. **Inform Candidates (If Applicable)**:

7. **Conduct a GDPR DPIA**:

### **7. Key Takeaways**
- Your obligations focus on **safe use, human oversight, input data quality, and transparency**, while the provider handles **design, conformity assessment, and technical documentation**.
- **Public authorities** have **additional obligations** (e.g., FRIA, registration).

### **1. Explicitly stated in retrieved text**
The **MDR** and **EU AI Act** both impose **serious-incident reporting duties** on manufacturers of high-risk AI systems that are also medical devices. However, the **AI Act explicitly defers to the MDR** for conformity assessment and post-market obligations, including vigilance reporting, when the AI system is a **safety component of, or itself, a product covered by Union harmonisation legislation listed in Annex I** (which includes the MDR).

- **AI Act Article 43(3)** states:
 > 

 This means the **MDR’s conformity assessment procedure** (including its vigilance reporting obligations) **applies**, and the **AI Act’s requirements are integrated into that procedure**.

- **MDR Article 87(1)** explicitly requires manufacturers to report:
 > 

 The MDR defines a **"serious incident"** (Article 2) as:
 > *"any malfunction or deterioration in the characteristics or performance of a device made available on the market, including use-error due to ergonomic features, as well as any inadequacy in the information supplied by the manufacturer and any undesirable side-effect."*

- **MDCG 2025-6 (non-binding guidance)** confirms this interaction:
 > 

 This reinforces that **MDR’s procedural framework (including vigilance reporting) takes precedence** when the AI system is a medical device.

---

### **2. Inference and likely outcome**
#### **(a) Actor status under both regulations**
- Under the **AI Act (Article 3(3))**, the **developer of the AI system is the "providermanufacturerplacing on the market" or "putting into serviceserious incident" overlap**:

 - **Likely interpretation**: Only **AI-specific incidents that pose a fundamental rights or safety risk** (e.g., bias leading to discrimination, cybersecurity breaches) must be reported under the AI Act **in addition to MDR reporting**.

2. **Role of notified bodies**:
 - Under **MDR**, notified bodies **review vigilance reports** (Article 92).

 - **Unclear**: Whether **MDR notified bodies will also assess AI-specific risks** or if this remains the responsibility of **AI market surveillance authorities**.

#### **(c) What would change the conclusion?**
- If the **AI system is not a safety component of a medical device** (e.g., it is a standalone AI tool used for administrative purposes in healthcare), **only the AI Act applies** (via Annex III).
- If the **medical device is Class I** (no third-party conformity assessment), the **AI system is not high-risk under the AI Act**, and **only MDR applies**.
- If the **incident is purely AI-related (e.g., data drift causing performance degradation) and does not affect the medical device’s safety or performance**, **only the AI Act’s reporting obligations apply**.
- If **future guidance (e.g., from the European Commission or MDCG) clarifies that AI-specific incidents must always be reported under both frameworks**, manufacturers would need to **duplicate reporting** (though this is unlikely given the AI Act’s deference to MDR).

---

### **Final Answer**
For a **high-risk AI system that is also a medical device under the MDR**, the **serious-incident reporting duties interact as follows**:

1. **MDR’s vigilance reporting (Articles 87–89) prevails** for all incidents involving the medical device, including its AI component. The manufacturer must report:

2. **AI Act’s post-market obligations (Articles 72–73) apply in parallel but supplement the MDR** for **AI-specific risks** (e.g., bias, robustness failures, cybersecurity breaches). The manufacturer must:
 - **Monitor AI-specific risks** continuously (Article 72).

### **1. Legal Definition of ‘AI System’ under the EU AI Act**
The **EU AI Act** defines an **‘AI system’** in **Article 3(1)** as follows:

> **"‘AI system’ means a machine-based system that is designed to operate with varying levels of autonomy and that may exhibit adaptiveness after deployment, and that, for explicit or implicit objectives, infers, from the input it receives, how to generate outputs such as predictions, content, recommendations, or decisions that can influence physical or virtual environments."**
> *[11] Article 3 — EU AI Act (CELEX: 32024R1689) [LEGISLATION] [BINDING]*

---

### **2. Key Distinguishing Features from Ordinary Software**
The **recital (12)** of the AI Act provides interpretive guidance on what sets an AI system apart from traditional software:

#### **(a) Capability to Infer**
- AI systems **derive models or algorithms from inputs or data** to generate outputs (e.g., predictions, decisions, or content).
- This goes beyond **rule-based execution** (e.g., "if X, then YThe capability to infer refers to the process of obtaining the outputs [...] and to a capability of AI systems to derive models or algorithms, or both, from inputs or data.AI systems are designed to operate with varying levels of autonomy, meaning that they have some degree of independence of actions from human involvement.The adaptiveness that an AI system could exhibit after deployment refers to self-learning capabilities, allowing the system to change while in use.The definition should not cover AI models used before their placing on the market for the sole purpose of research, development and prototyping activities."*
> *[16] (97) The notion of general-purpose AI models should be clearly defined and set a — EU AI Act (CELEX: 32024R1689) [LEGISLATION] [BINDING]*

---

### **4. Practical Implications for Medical Devices**
- **AI-enabled medical devices** (e.g., diagnostic imaging software, predictive algorithms) **must comply with both the MDR and the AI Act** if they meet the AI system definition.
- **Non-AI software** (e.g., static rule-based decision support tools) **remains subject only to the MDR**.
- **High-risk AI systems** (e.g., those used for medical diagnosis or treatment decisions) face **additional conformity assessment requirements** under the AI Act (e.g., risk management, data governance, transparency).

> **Key Takeaway:**
> An AI system under the EU AI Act is **not just software**—it is a **machine-based system that infers, adapts, and operates autonomously** to influence its environment. Traditional software lacks these inferential and adaptive capabilities.

---

Under **MDR 2017/745**, the **core obligations of a medical device manufacturer** are legally defined and binding. Below is a concise synthesis of the key obligations, grounded exclusively in the provided regulatory context:

---

### **1. Ensure Compliance with General Safety and Performance Requirements (GSPR)**
Manufacturers must ensure their devices **meet the GSPR set out in Annex I** before placing them on the market or putting them into service. This includes:
- **Design and manufacture** to achieve the intended performance and ensure safety during normal use ([1] Annex I, point 1):
 > 
- **Risk management** as a continuous, iterative process throughout the device lifecycle ([1] Annex I, point 3):
 > *"Manufacturers shall establish, implement, document and maintain a risk management system... requiring regular systematic updating."*
- **Clinical evaluation** to confirm conformity with GSPR, including benefit-risk assessment ([12] Chapter II, Article 5):
 > *"Demonstration of conformity with the general safety and performance requirements shall include a clinical evaluation in accordance with Article 61."*

**Cross-reference**: Article 61 requires clinical evaluation to confirm conformity with GSPR under normal use conditions.

---

### **2. Establish and Maintain a Quality Management System (QMS)**
Manufacturers must implement a **QMS proportionate to the risk class** of the device, covering:
- **Post-market surveillance (PMS)** as an integral part of the QMS ([13] Chapter VII, Article 83):
 > 
- **Vigilance reporting** for serious incidents and field safety corrective actions (FSCAs) via the electronic system in Article 92 ([3] Article 92, paragraph 1):
 > *"The Commission shall... set up and manage an electronic system to collate and process the following information: the reports by manufacturers on serious incidents and field safety corrective actions referred to in Article 87(1)."*
- **Trend reporting** for statistically significant increases in non-serious incidents or expected side-effects ([8] Article 88, paragraph 1):
 > *"Manufacturers shall report... any statistically significant increase in the frequency or severity of incidents that are not serious incidents or that are expected undesirable side-effects that could have a significant impact on the benefit-risk analysis."*

**Cross-reference**: Article 10(9) requires manufacturers to establish a QMS that includes PMS and vigilance.

---

### **3. Conduct Clinical Evaluation and Post-Market Clinical Follow-Up (PMCF)**
- **Clinical evaluation** must be **continuous, thorough, and objective**, based on clinical data (including equivalence claims where applicable) and documented in a **clinical evaluation report (CER)** ([11] Annex XIV, point 1):
 > *"To plan, continuously conduct and document a clinical evaluation, manufacturers shall: establish and update a clinical evaluation plan... analyse all relevant clinical data in order to reach conclusions about the safety and clinical performance of the device."*
- **PMCF** must be performed to update the clinical evaluation and address emerging risks ([11] Annex XIV, point 5):
 > *"PMCF shall be understood to be a continuous process that updates the clinical evaluation... with the aim of confirming the safety and performance throughout the expected lifetime of the device."*

**Cross-reference**: Article 61 requires clinical evaluation to confirm conformity with GSPR.

---

### **4. Implement Unique Device Identification (UDI) and Registration**
- **Assign and maintain UDI** for each device ([7] Annex VI, point 2.2):
 > *"The manufacturer shall assign and maintain unique UDIs for its devices."*
- **Register devices and economic operators** in the electronic system (Eudamed) and **periodically verify data accuracy** ([4] Annex VI, point 5.4):
 > *"Manufacturers shall periodically verify the correctness of all of the data relevant to devices they have placed on the market."*

**Cross-reference**: Article 29 requires registration of devices and economic operators in Eudamed.

---

### **5. Prepare and Maintain Technical Documentation**
Manufacturers must compile **technical documentation** (Annex II) and **PMS documentation** (Annex III), including:
- **Device description, intended purpose, and risk class** ([5] Annex IV, points 3–5).
- **Clinical evaluation report (CER)** and **PMCF plan/report** ([11] Annex XIV).
- **Post-market surveillance plan** ([13] Chapter VII, Article 84):
 > *"The post-market surveillance system... shall be based on a post-market surveillance plan, the requirements for which are set out in Section 1 of Annex III."*

**Cross-reference**: Article 10(4) requires manufacturers to draw up and keep technical documentation available for competent authorities.

---

### **6. Draw Up and Update the EU Declaration of Conformity**
Manufacturers must issue an **EU declaration of conformity** (Annex IV) containing:
- **Manufacturer’s details, Basic UDI-DI, device identification, risk class, and conformity statement** ([5] Annex IV, points 1–6):
 > *"The EU declaration of conformity shall contain all of the following information: Name, registered trade name or registered trade mark... A statement that the device that is covered by the present declaration is in conformity with this Regulation."*
- **References to harmonised standards or common specifications (CS) used** ([5] Annex IV, point 7).

---

### **7. Address Devices for Lay Persons (if applicable)**
For devices intended for use by lay persons, manufacturers must:
- **Design for safe and accurate use** by non-professionals, accounting for variations in technique and environment ([2] Annex I, point 22.1):
 > 
- **Include verification procedures** to confirm device performance at the time of use ([2] Annex I, point 22.3):
 > 

---

### **8. Notify Competent Authorities of Interruptions or Discontinuations**
Manufacturers must **inform competent authorities** if an interruption or discontinuation of supply could result in **serious harm to patients or public health** ([12] Chapter II, Article 10a, paragraph 1):
> 

---

### **Key Takeaways**
1. **Safety and performance** are non-negotiable and must be demonstrated through **risk management, clinical evaluation, and PMS**.
2. **Compliance is lifecycle-driven**: Obligations extend from pre-market design to post-market vigilance and PMCF.
3. **Documentation is critical**: Technical documentation, CER, PMS plan/report, and EU declaration of conformity must be **continuously updated**.
4. **Transparency and traceability** are enforced via **UDI, Eudamed registration, and vigilance reporting**.
5. **Lay person devices** require additional safeguards to mitigate user error and ensure safe use.

---

results.label: MDR Class IIb conformity assessment route (Annex IX vs Annex X)
results.reliance: Can be used as a strong first draft
results.answer:
### **Conformity Assessment Routes for Class IIb Medical Devices under MDR 2017/745**

#### **1. Primary Route: Annex IX (Quality Management System + Technical Documentation Assessment)**
- **Applicable Provision**: [2] Article 52(4) MDR
  > *"Manufacturers of class IIb devices, other than custom-made or investigational devices, shall be subject to a conformity assessment as specified in Chapters I and III of Annex IX, and including an assessment of the technical documentation as specified in Section 4 of that Annex of at least one representative device per generic device group."*

- **Key Requirements**:
  - The manufacturer must establish, document, and maintain a **quality management system (QMS)** in accordance with **Article 10(9)** and **Annex IX**.
  - The notified body assesses the QMS and conducts a **technical documentation review** (Annex IX, Section 4) for **at least one representative device per generic device group**.
  - The notified body issues a **certificate of conformity** (Article 56) if the device meets all applicable requirements.

- **Notified Body Role**:
  - **Audits the QMS** (Annex IX, Sections 2.3 and 2.4) to ensure compliance with MDR.
  - **Reviews technical documentation** (Annex II and III) for at least one representative device per generic device group.
  - **Performs surveillance** (Annex IX, Section 3) to ensure ongoing compliance.

---

#### **2. Alternative Route: Annex X (Type-Examination) + Annex XI (Production Quality Assurance or Product Verification)**
- **Applicable Provision**: [2] Article 52(3) MDR (by analogy, as this route is explicitly mentioned for Class III but may be chosen for Class IIb under certain conditions)
  > *"Alternatively, the manufacturer may choose to apply a conformity assessment as specified in Annex X coupled with a conformity assessment as specified in Annex XI."*

- **Key Requirements**:
  - **Annex X (Type-Examination)**:
    - The notified body examines a **representative sample** of the device and its technical documentation to verify compliance with MDR.
    - The notified body issues an **EU type-examination certificate** if the device meets requirements.
  - **Annex XI (Production Quality Assurance or Product Verification)**:
    - **Option A (Part A – Production Quality Assurance)**: The manufacturer ensures that production processes comply with the approved type (similar to Annex IX but without full QMS assessment).
    - **Option B (Part B – Product Verification)**: The notified body verifies conformity of each device or batch (less common for Class IIb due to scalability issues).

- **Notified Body Role**:
  - **Annex X**: Conducts **type-examination** (design and manufacturing review) and issues an **EU type-examination certificate**.
  - **Annex XI**:
    - For **Part A (Production Quality Assurance)**: Audits the manufacturer’s production processes to ensure consistency with the approved type.
    - For **Part B (Product Verification)**: Physically inspects and tests each device or batch (rarely used for Class IIb due to practical constraints).

---

### **Key Differences Between the Two Routes**
| **Aspect**               | **Annex IX (Primary Route)**                          | **Annex X + XI (Alternative Route)**                  |
|--------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|
| **Focus**                | Full QMS + technical documentation review            | Type-examination + production control                |
| **Notified Body Involvement** | Continuous (QMS audits + surveillance)          | Limited to type-examination + production checks      |
| **Flexibility**          | Better for large-scale production                    | Better for devices with stable designs               |
| **Documentation Burden** | Higher (full QMS + technical file)                   | Lower (focus on type + production)                   |

---

### **Notified Body’s Role in Both Routes**
1. **Pre-Market Assessment**:
   - Reviews **technical documentation** (Annex II and III) to verify compliance with **General Safety and Performance Requirements (GSPR, Annex I)**.
   - Assesses **clinical evaluation** (Article 61 and Annex XIV) and **risk management** (Annex I, Section 3).
   - Issues a **certificate of conformity** (Article 56) if the device meets all requirements.

2. **Post-Market Surveillance**:
   - Conducts **periodic audits** (Annex IX, Section 3) to ensure ongoing compliance.
   - Reviews **post-market clinical follow-up (PMCF)** data (Annex XIV, Part B) and **vigilance reports** (Article 87).

3. **Ongoing Compliance**:
   - Ensures the manufacturer maintains the **QMS** (for Annex IX) or **production controls** (for Annex XI).
   - May request **corrective actions** if non-conformities are identified.

---

### **Legal Effect of Conformity Assessment**
- Once the notified body issues a **certificate of conformity**, the manufacturer may:
  - **Draw up an EU declaration of conformity (Article 19)**.
  - **Affix the CE marking (Article 20)**.
  - **Place the device on the market (Article 5)**.

---

### **Interpretive Guidance (Non-Binding)**
While the **MDR is binding**, the following **MDCG guidance** (non-binding) provides operational clarity:
- **MDCG 2019-14**: Explains the **differences between Annex IX and Annex X/XI** and when each route may be preferable.
- **MDCG 2021-5**: Provides **practical examples** of how notified bodies assess technical documentation under Annex IX.
- **MDCG 2022-14**: Clarifies **surveillance requirements** for notified bodies under Annex IX.

**Caveat**: This guidance is **non-binding** and does not override the **MDR’s legal requirements**. Manufacturers must still comply with the **binding provisions of Article 52 and Annexes IX-XI**.

---

### **Conclusion**
- **Primary Route (Annex IX)**: Full QMS + technical documentation review (most common for Class IIb).
- **Alternative Route (Annex X + XI)**: Type-examination + production control (less common but available).
- **Notified Body Role**: Pre-market assessment (QMS and/or type-examination), post-market surveillance, and ongoing compliance verification.

---
> **⚠ Citation scope note:** The following citations are not present in the retrieved context for this question (scoped to MDR 2017/745): Annex IX, Annex X, Annex XI, Article 56, Article 61, Article 87. Please verify against the source provisions.

### **Direct Answer** An **in vitro diagnostic (IVD) device** is classified as **Class B** or **Class C** under **IVDR 2017/746** based on its **intended purpose** and the **risk it poses to patients or public health**, as defined in **Annex VIII (Classification Rules)**. The classification determines whether a **notified body** must be involved in the conformity assessment: - **Class B devices** (low to moderate risk) **do not require notified body involvement** for conformity assessment, except for **sterile Class B devices**, which require notified body assessment of sterility aspects. - **Class C devices** (moderate to high risk) **require notified body involvement** for conformity assessment under **Article 48(7–8)**. --- ### **1. Classification Rules (Annex VIII)** The **IVDR uses a rule-based system** in **Annex VIII** to classify IVDs into **Class A (lowest risk), B, C, or D (highest risk)**. The classification is **governed by the device’s intended purpose** ([6] Annex VIII, Point 1.1): > *"Application of the classification rules shall be governed by the intended purpose of the devices."* #### **Key Rules for Class B vs. Class C** | **Rule** | **Class** | **Criteria** | **Examples** | |----------|----------|-------------|-------------| | **Rule 3** | **Class B** | Devices **not covered by higher rules** (e.g., general lab reagents, specimen receptacles, culture media). | - General chemistry reagents
- Specimen collection containers
- Non-automated urinalysis strips | | **Rule 4** | **Class C** | Devices for **detecting or monitoring moderate-risk conditions** where an incorrect result could lead to **misdiagnosis or delayed treatment**, but **not life-threatening**. | - **Self-tests** (e.g., pregnancy, glucose monitoring)
- **Infectious disease tests** (e.g., Chlamydia, HIV screening)
- **Genetic testing** (e.g., cystic fibrosis carrier screening)
- **Tumor markers** (e.g., PSA, CA-125)
- **Blood glucose monitoring systems** (for diabetes management) | | **Rule 5** | **Class C** | Devices for **determining compatibility of blood, cells, or tissues** (except for **ABO, Rh, Kell, Duffy, Kidd systems**, which are **Class D**). | - **HLA typing** (for organ transplantation)
- **Minor blood group typing** (e.g., Lewis, MNS) | | **Rule 6** | **Class C** | Devices for **detecting congenital disorders** where a false result could lead to **high individual risk** (e.g., Down syndrome screening). | - **Prenatal screening tests** (e.g., first-trimester combined screening)
- **Newborn screening** (e.g., phenylketonuria, congenital hypothyroidism) | #### **Higher Classification Overrides Lower Rules** - If **multiple rules apply**, the **highest class applies** ([6] Annex VIII, Point 1.9): > *"If several classification rules apply to the same device, the rule resulting in the higher classification shall apply."* - **Example**: A **self-test for HIV** (Rule 4 → **Class C**) overrides a general reagent classification (Rule 3 → **Class B**). --- ### **2. When is a Notified Body Required? (Article 48)** The **conformity assessment procedure** (and thus **notified body involvement**) depends on the **device class**: | **Class** | **Notified Body Involvement?** | **Conformity Assessment Procedure** | **Key Provisions** | |-----------|-------------------------------|------------------------------------|-------------------| | **Class A (non-sterile)** | ❌ **No** | **Manufacturer’s self-declaration** (Annex III technical documentation) | [2] Article 48(10) | | **Class A (sterile)** | ✅ **Yes** (for sterility aspects) | **Annex IX (QMS) + Annex XI (sterility batch release)** | [2] Article 48(10) | | **Class B** | ❌ **No** (unless sterile) | **Manufacturer’s self-declaration** (Annex III technical documentation) | [2] Article 48(10) | | **Class B (sterile)** | ✅ **Yes** (for sterility aspects) | **Annex IX (QMS) + Annex XI (sterility batch release)** | [2] Article 48(10) | | **Class C** | ✅ **Yes** | **Annex IX (QMS + technical documentation) OR Annex X (type-examination) + Annex XI (production QA)** | [2] Article 48(7–8) | | **Class D** | ✅ **Yes** (highest scrutiny) | **Annex IX (QMS + EU reference lab testing) OR Annex X + Annex XI** | [2] Article 48(3–4) | #### **Key Provisions for Class C** - **Article 48(7)** ([2]) requires **notified body assessment** for **Class C devices**: > *"Manufacturers of class C devices, other than devices for performance study, shall be subject to a conformity assessment as specified in Chapters I and III of Annex IX, and, in addition, to an assessment of the technical documentation as specified in Section 4 of that Annex for at least one representative device per generic device group."* - **Alternative route (Article 48(8))**: > *"Manufacturers of class C devices, other than devices for performance study, may, instead of the conformity assessment procedure pursuant to paragraph 7, choose to apply a conformity assessment as specified in Annex X coupled with a conformity assessment as specified in Annex XI except its Section 5."* --- ### **3. Summary: Class B vs. Class C & Notified Body Involvement** | **Aspect** | **Class B** | **Class C** | |------------|------------|------------| | **Risk Level** | Low to moderate | Moderate to high | | **Examples** | - General lab reagents
- Specimen containers
- Non-automated urinalysis strips | - Self-tests (pregnancy, glucose)
- Infectious disease screening (HIV, Chlamydia)
- Genetic testing (carrier screening)
- Tumor markers (PSA, CA-125)
- Blood glucose monitoring systems | | **Notified Body Required?** | ❌ **No** (unless sterile) | ✅ **Yes** | | **Conformity Assessment** | **Self-declaration (Annex III)** | **Annex IX (QMS + technical doc) OR Annex X + Annex XI** | | **Key IVDR Provisions** | [2] Article 48(10) | [2] Article 48(7–8) | --- ### **4. Legal Interpretation & Practical Considerations** - **Intended purpose is decisive**: The **manufacturer’s stated intended use** (as per **Article 2(12)** and **Annex II, Section 1.1**) determines classification. If a device could be used for **both Class B and Class C purposes**, the **higher class applies** ([6] Annex VIII, Point 1.8). - **Software classification**: If the software is **independent of a hardware IVD**, it is **classified in its own right** ([6] Annex VIII, Point 1.4). For example: - A **standalone AI-based diagnostic algorithm** for **tumor marker interpretation** would likely be **Class C** (Rule 4). - A **general lab data management software** might be **Class B** (Rule 3). - **Borderline cases**: Some devices (e.g., **rapid antigen tests for COVID-19**) may fall under **Rule 1 (Class D)** if they detect a **life-threatening pathogen with high propagation risk**, but **Rule 4 (Class C)** if used for **general screening**. The **manufacturer must justify the classification** in the **technical documentation (Annex II)**. --- ### **5. Key Takeaways for Compliance** 1. **Classify first**: Use **Annex VIII rules** to determine if the device is **Class B or C**. 2. **Check sterility**: If the device is **sterile**, even **Class B requires notified body involvement** for sterility aspects. 3. **Class C always requires a notified body**: No exceptions. 4. **Document the justification**: The **technical file (Annex II)** must include the **classification rationale**. 5. **Software is classified separately**: If the software is **independent**, apply **Annex VIII, Rule 1.4**. Would you like a **specific example** (e.g., a glucose monitor or genetic test) analyzed in detail? --- > **⚠ Citation scope note:** The following citations are not present in the retrieved context for this question (scoped to IVDR 2017/746): Annex III, Annex IX, Annex X, Annex XI, Article 2(12). Please verify against the source provisions.

### **Direct Answer**
A hospital (health institution) may manufacture and use a device in-house under **MDR Article 5(5)** if it meets **all eight cumulative conditions** listed in that provision. Even when exempt from most MDR requirements, the hospital **must still comply with the general safety and performance requirements (GSPR) in Annex I** and maintain documentation to demonstrate conformity.

---

### **Conditions for the Health Institution Exemption (Article 5(5))**
The exemption applies **only if all of the following conditions are met** (verbatim from [1] Article 5(5)):

1. **No transfer to another legal entity**
 *"the devices are not transferred to another legal entity"*
 → The device must remain within the same health institution; it cannot be sold or supplied to another organisation.

2. **Quality management system (QMS)**
 *"manufacture and use of the devices occur under appropriate quality management systems"*
 → The hospital must implement a QMS covering design, manufacturing, and use (e.g., ISO 13485 or equivalent).

3. **Justification of unmet clinical need**
 *"the health institution justifies in its documentation that the target patient group's specific needs cannot be met, or cannot be met at the appropriate level of performance by an equivalent device available on the market"*
 → The hospital must document why no commercially available device meets the patient’s needs (e.g., rare conditions, custom anatomical requirements).

4. **Competent authority reporting**
 *"the health institution provides information upon request on the use of such devices to its competent authority, which shall include a justification of their manufacturing, modification and use"*
 → The hospital must be prepared to disclose details to regulators upon request.

5. **Public declaration**
 
 → The hospital must publish a declaration (e.g., on its website) with the above details.

6. **Technical documentation**
 
 → The hospital must maintain comprehensive documentation (e.g., design files, risk management, clinical justification).

7. **Manufacturing compliance**
 
 → Manufacturing must align with the documented specifications.

8. **Post-market surveillance and corrective actions**
 *"the health institution reviews experience gained from clinical use of the devices and takes all necessary corrective actions"*
 → The hospital must monitor device performance and implement corrective actions (e.g., recalls, design changes) if issues arise.

---

### **Obligations That Still Apply**
Even if all conditions are met, the hospital **must comply with**:
- **Annex I (GSPR)**: The device must meet the general safety and performance requirements (e.g., risk management, clinical benefit, labelling).
 ([1] Article 5(5)).

- **Clinical evaluation**: While not explicitly stated in Article 5(5), the GSPR (Annex I) requires clinical evaluation to confirm safety and performance. This aligns with the definition of *"clinical evaluationdemonstration of conformity with the general safety and performance requirements shall include a clinical evaluation in accordance with Article 61."*

---

### **Key Limitations**
1. **No commercialisation**: The exemption **does not permit placing the device on the market** or transferring it to another entity ([1] Article 5(5)(a)).
2. **No exemption from GSPR**: The hospital must still ensure the device is safe and effective ([1] Article 5(5)).
3. **Case-by-case justification**: The hospital must document why no equivalent commercial device exists ([1] Article 5(5)(c)).

---

### **Regulatory Reasoning**
- The exemption is **narrowly scoped** to address unmet clinical needs where no suitable commercial device exists.
- The hospital assumes **manufacturer-like responsibilities** (e.g., QMS, risk management, post-market surveillance) but without the full MDR conformity assessment.
- The **GSPR (Annex I)** remains binding because it is the **minimum safety standard** for all medical devices, regardless of origin.

---

### **Direct Answer**
Under **MDR Article 120(3c), point (b)**, a **‘significant change in the design or intended purpose’** of a legacy device (CE-marked under MDD/AIMDD) **terminates the transitional benefit** and requires full MDR certification before the device may be further placed on the market. The assessment is **case-by-case**, but the following **binding legal criteria** and **non-binding MDCG guidance** determine significance:

---

### **Step 1 – Legal Definition & Trigger EventBinding Regulation (MDR Article 120(3c), point (b)) [BINDING]**
> 

- **Trigger event**: Any change to a legacy device **after 26 May 2021** (MDR date of application).
- **Legal consequence**: If the change is **significant**, the device **loses its transitional benefit** and must undergo full MDR conformity assessment.

---

### **Step 2 – Two Cumulative Elements (Binding + Guidance)Binding Regulation (MDR Article 120(3c), point (b)) [BINDING]**
> *"significant changes in the design or intended purpose"*

**MDCG 2020-3 Rev.1 (non-binding) [GUIDANCE]**
> 

- **Element 1**: The change **must affect either the design or the intended purpose** (not administrative or manufacturing process changes).
- **Element 2**: The change **must be significant** (assessed via MDCG flowcharts).

Manufacturers must ultimately justify their assessments based on the binding provisions of MDR Article 120(3c)."*

### **1. Post-Market Surveillance (PMS) ObligationsConclusion:** The manufacturer must establish, implement, and maintain a **proportionate, risk-based PMS system** as an integral part of its quality management system (QMS). This system must **proactively collect, record, and analyse data** on device quality, performance, and safety throughout its lifetime, and **trigger corrective/preventive actions** when necessary.

#### **Key Obligations:(a) PMS System (Article 83, Chapter VII)**
- The PMS system must be **proportionate to the risk class** and **appropriate for the device type**.
- It must **actively and systematically** gather, record, and analyse data on:
 - **Quality, performance, and safety** of the device.
 - **Adverse events, complaints, and trends** (including non-serious incidents).
 - **User feedback** (e.g., from lay persons, healthcare professionals).
- The system must **draw conclusions** and **determine, implement, and monitor** preventive/corrective actions.
 > **[13] Article 83, Paragraph 1(b) PMS Plan (Article 84, Annex III)**
- The PMS system must be **based on a documented PMS plan**, which is part of the **technical documentation** (Annex II) for all devices **except custom-made devices**.
- The plan must address:
 - **Data collection methods** (e.g., complaints, literature, registries, user feedback).
 - **Data analysis** (e.g., trend reporting, benefit-risk assessment).
 - **Reporting obligations** (e.g., serious incidents, field safety corrective actions (FSCAs)).
 - **Post-market clinical follow-up (PMCF)** (if applicable).
 > *"The post-market surveillance system referred to in Article 83 shall be based on a post-market surveillance plan, the requirements for which are set out in Section 1 of Annex III."* **[13] Article 84(c) PMS Reporting (Articles 87–92)**
- **Serious incidents** (Article 87) and **FSCAs** (Article 89) must be reported via the **electronic system (Eudamed)** (Article 92).
- **Trend reporting** (Article 88) is required for **statistically significant increases** in:
 - **Non-serious incidents** or **expected undesirable side-effects** that impact the **benefit-risk ratio**.
 - **Unacceptable risks** to health/safety.
 > **[8] Article 88, Paragraph 1**

- **Periodic Safety Update Reports (PSURs)** (Article 86):
 - **Class IIa, IIb, and III devices**: PSURs must be **updated at least annually** (or as specified by the notified body).
 - **Class I devices**: A **PMS report** (simplified PSUR) must be prepared and updated as needed, available upon request.
 > **[13] Article 85**

---

### **2. Post-Market Clinical Follow-Up (PMCF) ObligationsConclusion:** PMCF is a **continuous process** that **updates the clinical evaluation** (Annex XIV) by **proactively collecting clinical data** from real-world use of CE-marked devices. It is **mandatory** unless justified otherwise.

#### **Key Obligations:(a) PMCF as Part of Clinical Evaluation (Annex XIV, Part B)**
- PMCF must be **conducted pursuant to a documented PMCF plan** (Annex XIV, Point 6).
- It aims to:
 - **Confirm safety and performance** throughout the device’s expected lifetime.
 - **Ensure continued acceptability of identified risks**.
 - **Detect emerging risks** based on factual evidence.
 > *"PMCF shall be understood to be a continuous process that updates the clinical evaluation referred to in Article 61 and Part A of this Annex and shall be addressed in the manufacturer's post-market surveillance plan."* **[11] Annex XIV, Point 5(b) PMCF Plan (Annex XIV, Point 6.1–6.2)**
The PMCF plan must specify:
- **Methods/procedures** for proactive data collection (e.g., registries, surveys, clinical studies).
- **Objectives** (e.g., confirming long-term safety, identifying rare adverse events).
- **Evaluation criteria** (e.g., clinical endpoints, statistical methods).
- **Timelines** for data collection and reporting.
 > **[11] Annex XIV, Point 6.1(c) PMCF Evaluation Report (Annex XIV, Point 7–8)**
- Findings must be **documented in a PMCF evaluation report**, which is part of the:
 - **Clinical evaluation report (CER)**.
 - **Technical documentation**.
- The report must include:
 - **Analysis of PMCF data**.
 - **Conclusions on safety/performance**.
 - **Impact on benefit-risk ratio**.
 - **Preventive/corrective actions** (if needed).
 > *"The manufacturer shall analyse the findings of the PMCF and document the results in a PMCF evaluation report that shall be part of the clinical evaluation report and the technical documentation."* **[11] Annex XIV, Point 7**

---

### **3. Interaction Between PMS and PMCF**
- **PMS** is the **overarching system** for **all post-market data** (clinical, non-clinical, complaints, vigilance).
- **PMCF** is a **subset of PMS**, focusing **specifically on clinical data** to update the **clinical evaluation**.
- **PMCF data feeds into PMS**, and **PMS findings may trigger PMCF activities** (e.g., if new risks emerge).

---

### **4. Key Differences: PMS vs. PMCF**
| **Aspect** | **Post-Market Surveillance (PMS)** | **Post-Market Clinical Follow-Up (PMCF)** |
|--------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------|
| **Scope** | All post-market data (clinical, non-clinical, complaints). | **Only clinical data** (safety/performance in real-world use). |
| **Purpose** | Ensure ongoing compliance, detect risks, trigger actions. | **Update clinical evaluation**, confirm long-term safety. |
| **Regulatory Basis** | Article 83–92, Annex III. | Annex XIV, Part B. |
| **Output** | PMS report (Class I), PSUR (Class IIa/IIb/III), vigilance reports. | PMCF evaluation report (part of CER). |
| **Frequency** | Continuous (with periodic reporting). | Continuous (plan-based, with defined timelines). |

---

### **5. Practical Steps for Compliance**
1. **Develop a PMS Plan** (Annex III) as part of the **technical documentation**.
2. **Implement a PMS System** (Article 83) integrated into the **QMS** (Article 10(9)).
3. **Monitor and Report**:
 - **Serious incidents** (Article 87) and **FSCAs** (Article 89) via **Eudamed** (Article 92).
 - **Trends** (Article 88) for non-serious incidents/undesirable side-effects.
 - **PSURs** (Article 86) or **PMS reports** (Article 85) as applicable.
4. **Conduct PMCF** (Annex XIV) unless justified otherwise, and **update the clinical evaluation** accordingly.
5. **Review and Update** PMS/PMCF plans **continuously** based on new data.

---

### **6. Exemptions and Justifications**
- **PMCF is not mandatory** if the manufacturer can **justify** that:
 - The device has **well-established safety/performance** (e.g., long-term market history).
 - **No new clinical data is needed** to confirm ongoing compliance.
 - **Alternative data sources** (e.g., literature, registries) are sufficient.
 > *"Justifications in relation to non-performance of clinical investigations or PMCF"* **[10] Annex VII, Subsection 4.5.5 (bullet point)**

- **However**, the **PMS system must always be maintained**, regardless of PMCF status.

---

### **Direct Answer**
Under **MDR 2017/745**, importers and distributors share core traceability and vigilance obligations, but importers bear **additional pre-market verification and registration duties** that distributors do not. The key differences are:

1. **Pre-market verification** – Importers must verify CE marking, EU declaration of conformity, manufacturer identification, labelling, instructions for use, and UDI assignment before placing a device on the market ([2] Article 13(2)). Distributors only verify these before *making available* on the market ([2] Article 14(2)).
2. **Registration in Eudamed** – Importers must register themselves in the electronic system and add their details to device registrations ([2] Article 13(4), [7] Article 31(1)). Distributors have no such obligation.
3. **Labelling requirement** – Importers must indicate their name, registered trade name/trademark, and address on the device, packaging, or accompanying document ([2] Article 13(3)). Distributors only do so if they relabel or repackage ([3] Article 16(3)).
4. **Complaints and non-conformities** – Both must forward complaints to the manufacturer, but importers must also inform the manufacturer’s authorised representative ([2] Article 13(8)), while distributors inform the manufacturer, authorised representative, *and* importer ([2] Article 14(5)).
5. **Assumption of manufacturer obligations** – Both may assume manufacturer obligations if they modify a device’s intended purpose or affect compliance ([3] Article 16(1)), but importers are more likely to trigger this due to their pre-market role.

---

### **Key Provisions and Verbatim Quotes**

#### **1. Pre-market verification**
- **Importer obligation** ([2] Article 13(2)):
 > 

- **Distributor obligation** ([2] Article 14(2)):
 > 

**Difference**: Importers verify *before placing on the market* (i.e., at the EU border), while distributors verify *before making available* (i.e., before sale to end-users).

---

#### **2. Registration in Eudamed**
- **Importer obligation** ([2] Article 13(4)):
 > *"Importers shall verify that the device is registered in the electronic system in accordance with Article 29. Importers shall add their details to the registration in accordance with Article 31."*

- **Distributor obligation**: *None*. Distributors are not required to register in Eudamed ([7] Article 31 applies only to manufacturers, authorised representatives, and importers).

---

#### **3. Labelling requirement**
- **Importer obligation** ([2] Article 13(3)):
 > *"Importers shall indicate on the device or on its packaging or in a document accompanying the device their name, registered trade name or registered trade mark, their registered place of business and the address at which they can be contacted, so that their location can be established."*

- **Distributor obligation**: Only if relabelling/repackaging ([3] Article 16(3)):
 > *"A distributor or importer that carries out any of the activities mentioned in points (a) and (b) of paragraph 2 shall indicate on the device or, where that is impracticable, on its packaging or in a document accompanying the device, the activity carried out together with its name, registered trade name or registered trade mark, registered place of business and the address at which it can be contacted."*

**Difference**: Importers must label *all* devices they place on the market; distributors only label if they modify packaging/translation.

---

#### **4. Complaints and non-conformities**
- **Importer obligation** ([2] Article 13(8)):
 > 

- **Distributor obligation** ([2] Article 14(5)):
 > 

**Difference**: Distributors must also inform the importer; importers do not.

---

#### **5. Assumption of manufacturer obligations**
- **Both importers and distributors** may assume manufacturer obligations if they:
 - Place a device on the market under their own name/trademark ([3] Article 16(1)(a)).
 - Change the intended purpose of a device ([3] Article 16(1)(b)).
 - Modify a device in a way that may affect compliance ([3] Article 16(1)(c)).

**Difference**: Importers are more likely to trigger this due to their role in placing devices on the market (e.g., rebranding or modifying devices from third countries).

---

### **Summary Table**
| **Obligation** | **Importer** | **Distributor** |
|------------------------------|---------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Pre-market verification | Before *placing on market* | Before *making available* |
| Eudamed registration | Required ([2] Art 13(4)) | Not required |
| Labelling | Mandatory for all devices | Only if relabelling/repackaging |
| Complaints reporting | To manufacturer + authorised rep | To manufacturer + authorised rep + importer |
| Assumption of manufacturer obligations | More likely (e.g., rebranding) | Less likely (unless modifying device) |

---

### **Legal Inference**
The MDR distinguishes importers and distributors based on their **position in the supply chain**:
- Importers act as the **first economic operator in the EU**, requiring stricter pre-market controls to ensure devices from third countries comply with MDR.
- Distributors act **downstream**, focusing on post-market vigilance and traceability.

Both must cooperate with manufacturers to maintain traceability ([1] Article 25), but importers bear **additional gatekeeping responsibilities** to prevent non-compliant devices from entering the EU market.

### **1. Direct Answer**
Under **MDR Article 11**, the **authorised representative** (AR) of a non-EU manufacturer is a **legally mandated intermediary** with **specific obligations** but **no delegation of core manufacturer responsibilities**. The AR’s role is **operational and compliance-focused**, ensuring the manufacturer meets MDR requirements while remaining jointly and severally liable for defective devices if the manufacturer fails to comply.

---

### **2. Key Obligations of the Authorised Representative**

#### **(A) Mandatory Designation and Scope**
- The AR **must be designated in writing** by the manufacturer and **accept the mandate** ([2] Article 11(2)).
 > *"The designation shall constitute the authorised representative's mandate, it shall be valid only when accepted in writing by the authorised representative and shall be effective at least for all devices of the same generic device group."*
- The AR is **sole** (no multiple ARs for the same device group) and **must be established in the EU** ([2] Article 11(1)).

#### **(B) Tasks Specified in the Mandate**
The AR **shall perform the tasks specified in the mandate** agreed with the manufacturer ([2] Article 11(3)).
- **Examples of typical tasks** (non-exhaustive, as per the mandate):
 - Acting as the **contact point for EU authorities** (e.g., competent authorities, notified bodies).
 - **Providing the mandate** to competent authorities upon request ([2] Article 11(3)).
 - **Facilitating conformity assessment procedures** (e.g., liaising with notified bodies).
 - **Ensuring technical documentation is available** for inspection by authorities ([8] Article 10(14) cross-references AR’s role in Article 11(7)).

#### **(C) Legal Liability for Defective Devices**
- The AR is **jointly and severally liable** with the manufacturer for defective devices **if the manufacturer fails to comply with MDR obligations** ([2] Article 11(5)).
 > *"Without prejudice to paragraph 4 of this Article, where the manufacturer is not established in a Member State and has not complied with the obligations laid down in Article 10, the authorised representative shall be legally liable for defective devices on the same basis as, and jointly and severally with, the manufacturer."*
- **Liability is triggered only if**:
 1. The manufacturer is **non-EU** and **non-compliant** with Article 10 obligations (e.g., risk management, clinical evaluation, QMS, PMS).
 2. The AR **fails to act** on its mandate (e.g., not informing authorities of non-compliance).

#### **(D) Termination of Mandate**
- If the AR terminates the mandate due to the manufacturer’s **non-compliance**, it must **immediately inform**:
 - The **competent authority** of its Member State.
 - The **notified body** (if involved in conformity assessment) ([2] Article 11(6)).
 > *"An authorised representative who terminates its mandate on the ground referred to in point (h) of paragraph 3 shall immediately inform the competent authority... and, where applicable, the notified body..."*

#### **(E) Non-Delegable Manufacturer Obligations**
- The AR **cannot** be mandated to perform the manufacturer’s **core obligations** under Article 10, including:
 - **Design/manufacturing compliance** ([8] Article 10(1)).
 - **Risk management** ([8] Article 10(2)).
 - **Clinical evaluation** ([8] Article 10(3)).
 - **Technical documentation** ([8] Article 10(4)).
 - **UDI/registration** ([8] Article 10(11)).
 - **Post-market surveillance (PMS)** ([8] Article 10(10)).
 > ([2] Article 11(4))

---

### **3. Cross-Regulation Awareness: GDPR Overlap**
- The AR’s role under MDR is **distinct from a GDPR "representativegatekeeperregulatory shield"**: The manufacturer remains **primarily responsible** for compliance.
2. **AR’s liability is conditional**: Only arises if the manufacturer **fails to comply** and the AR **does not act**.
3. **Mandate must be precise**: Clearly define tasks (e.g., vigilance reporting, authority liaison) to avoid ambiguity.
4. **Document everything**: Maintain records of the mandate, communications, and compliance checks to demonstrate due diligence.

---

- **Clinical evaluation** (Article 61, Annex XIV) is mandatory for all MDSW, including standalone CDSS. This involves:

> ℹ️ **Wording check** — 2 quote(s) are near-verbatim (grounded in the source but with minor wording differences). Verify exact wording before quoting them externally.
> - “the natural or legal person … which, alone or jointly with others, **determines the purposes and means** of the proce...”
> - “a natural or legal person … which **processes personal data on behalf of the controller**.”

**Direct Answer:**
The cloud vendor is **a processor** under the GDPR, provided it processes personal data **only on the hospital’s documented instructions** and does not determine the purposes or means of the processing. The decisive factor is **who determines the purposes and means of processing**—the hospital (controller) or the vendor (processor).

---

**Regulatory Reasoning:**

1. **Legal Definitions (Article 4 GDPR)**
   - **Controller** is defined as “the natural or legal person … which, alone or jointly with others, **determines the purposes and means** of the processing of personal data.” [1] Article 4(7)
   - **Processor** is defined as “a natural or legal person … which **processes personal data on behalf of the controller**.” [1] Article 4(8)

2. **Processor Obligations (Article 28 GDPR)**
   - A processor must process personal data **only on documented instructions** from the controller (Article 28(3)(a)).
   - If the vendor goes beyond those instructions and determines its own purposes or means, it becomes a **controller** for that processing (Article 28(10)).

3. **Factual Determination**
   - The hospital decides **why** (diagnostic purpose) and **how** (which AI model, which data fields) the data are processed.
   - The vendor merely **executes** the inference on the data it receives, without altering the hospital’s purposes or means.
   - Therefore, the vendor is a **processor** under the GDPR.
